text: During a total laparoscopic hysterectomy on an obese patient, the surgeon navigates the fatty bowels to maintain a clear view of the operative field. The left round ligament is cut, and dissection proceeds lateral to the eye pivot. The surgeon skeletonizes the vessel before coagulation and sectioning, ensuring safe control. The peritoneum is removed, and a window is created below the IP one following the Clermont-Ferrand technique. The vessel is sealed over three centimeters, allowing safe cutting of the IP.

The surgeon identifies the dissection plane between the bladder and vagina, with assistants pulling the uterus towards the sacrum. The bladder is retracted to develop the vesicular vagina. Dissection continues towards the right round ligament, which is controlled, and progresses towards the IP. The bowel is repeatedly lifted to maintain a clear view, highlighting the challenge in obese patients. The right round ligament is skeletonized after visualizing the right ureter, with the bowel close to the ureter requiring frequent repositioning.

The surgeon maintains a pneumoperitoneum of ten millimeters due to patient obesity. The posterior leaf of the broad ligament is cut, and the right uterine pedicle is controlled by sealing the vessel without cutting to reduce backflow. The uterine artery is sealed at three centimeters but remains uncut. The left uterine pedicle is controlled after sectioning the posterior leaf of the broad ligament, with careful skeletonization of vessels due to the patient's condition. The dissection progresses towards the upper part of the left uterosacral ligament, reaching the posterior valve of the uterine manipulator.

The surgeon performs vesico-vascular dissection, cutting both medial aspects of the large bladder. A lateral dissection is done to pull down the bladder without tension. The left uterine artery is cut, and dissection continues medial to it to control the cervical vaginal vessels. The bowel is visible close to the operative field. A stitch is placed through the bladder peritoneum to elevate the bladder, improving the view of the vagina. A monocular hook in coagulating mode is used for dissection.

The surgeon cuts the lateral and posterior vaginal aspects for improved hemostasis, facilitating the extraction of the small uterus. The bladder pexy is visible, aiding in the procedure as the assistant pulls up the bowel. The surgeon begins closing the vagina using an autoblock knot, inserting the needle through the entire mucosa and residual utero-sacral ligament to reduce the risk of vaginal prolapse.

The surgeon uses an intracorporeal knotting technique for vaginal closure, while the assistant's hand is visible pulling up the bowel to maintain a clear operative field. The surgeon controls hemostasis and cleans the pelvis during the total laparoscopic hysterectomy in an obese patient.
